tldr: During the quarter, this cohort of elite managers exhibited a clear rotation toward semiconductor infrastructure and financial data moats while actively trimming exposure to consumer discretionary and select mega-cap tech names. The consensus moves highlight a strategic pivot into the 'picks and shovels' of the technology sector via chipmakers and industry ETFs, alongside a flight to quality in high-margin payment and ratings monopolies. Conversely, managers took profits or reduced risk in high-multiple consumer growth stories and heavily sold Microsoft, reflecting a more selective approach to market leadership.

themes.heading: Leaning Into AI Hardware & Semiconductors
themes.detail: Managers aggressively initiated and added to semiconductor manufacturers and broad industry ETFs, signaling a preference for the foundational hardware layer of the tech sector. SMH saw 3 initiations and 3 buys, while individual chipmakers like Broadcom, Micron, and Taiwan Semiconductor were also accumulated.

themes.heading: Flight to Financial Data & Payment Moats
themes.detail: There was strong consensus buying in wide-moat financial services, specifically payment networks and credit rating/data oligopolies. Visa led the pack with 4 buys (including 2 initiations), supported by additions to S&P Global and Moody's.

themes.heading: Trimming Consumer Discretionary & High-Multiple Growth
themes.detail: The cohort leaned out of consumer-facing names, airlines, and high-growth e-commerce, suggesting caution around consumer spending. Target, Chipotle, MercadoLibre, Rocket Companies, and American Airlines each saw selling pressure from 3 distinct managers.

themes.heading: Mega-Cap Tech Divergence
themes.detail: Managers showed mixed sentiment in mega-cap tech, heavily selling Microsoft (the most-sold stock with 5 sellers) while actively trading Amazon and Alphabet on both the buy and sell sides. Despite the mixed trading, Alphabet and Amazon remain the most widely held names across the roster.
